channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: From Recombination to the CMB
item.description:
For the first 380,000 years, the universe was opaque. Photons could not travel more than a short distance before scattering off free electrons. Then, in a geologically brief period, the universe became transparent. The light released in that transition is still visible today.

The opaque universe

After nucleosynthesis, the universe was a hot plasma: atomic nuclei (mostly hydrogen and helium), free electrons, and photons, all tightly coupled. Photons scattered incessantly off free electrons – a process called Thomson scattering. The mean free path of a photon was short. The universe was like the interior of a star: luminous but opaque, because light could not travel in a straight line.

item.title: The Accelerating Expansion
item.description:
In 1998, two teams of astronomers measuring distant supernovae found something nobody expected: the expansion of the universe is speeding up.

The expectation

For most of the 20th century, cosmologists assumed the expansion of the universe was decelerating. Gravity attracts. Every bit of matter in the universe pulls on every other bit. This mutual attraction should slow the expansion over time, like a ball thrown upward. The open question was not whether expansion was decelerating, but by how much – and whether it would eventually halt and reverse.

item.title: The Expanding Universe
item.description:
In 1929, Edwin Hubble observed that distant galaxies are moving away from us, and the farther they are, the faster they recede. This was the first evidence that the universe is expanding.

What expansion means

The expansion of the universe is not galaxies flying apart through space like shrapnel from a bomb. It is space itself growing. The standard analogy is raisin bread rising in an oven: the raisins (galaxies) are not moving through the dough (space), but the dough is expanding, carrying the raisins along. From any raisin’s perspective, all other raisins are moving away, and the more distant ones are receding faster.

item.title: The First Three Minutes
item.description:
Run the expansion backward. The universe gets hotter and denser. At some point, it was hot enough for nuclear fusion – not inside stars, but everywhere, all at once. This text traces the first few minutes of the universe, when the light elements were forged.

Before nucleosynthesis

The standard Big Bang model describes the universe as beginning in an extremely hot, dense state. The model does not describe the very first instant – the “singularity” at t=0t = 0 is a signal that general relativity breaks down. But from about 10−610^{-6} seconds onward, the physics is well understood.
